data.title: Chipotle: Rare Opportunity To Buy At A Truly Attractive Price
data.description:
You don't need me to tell you that Chipotle (NYSE:CMG) is the poster child of a successful investment in the quick-service restaurant sector.

In the seven years following the notorious e-coli outbreak, Chipotle's stock rose more than 10x, sales grew 2.5x, units grew over 50%, and margins more than tripled. All that, and there's still significantly more runway.

And now, we get a rare opportunity to buy at a truly attractive price. Let's dive in.

Introduction To Chipotle & Revisiting The Investment Thesis

When it comes to investing in restaurant companies, there's really no need to overcomplicate things. It all comes down to three questions.

One, does a store have good unit economics, represented by same-store growth, restaurant-level margins, and return on invested capital?

Two, are those good unit economics replicable so that you can duplicate them across the country?

Three, is there more space to open more locations, or is the market already saturated so that you either have to expand into riskier geographies, or cannibalize existing stores?

In my view, Chipotle is among a very short list of companies that provide the whole package and meet all three of those key questions, with industry-leading AUV of over $3.2 million, restaurant margins in the high-20%, and a footprint that's half the size of most mature restaurant chains.

Although Chipotle's fundamentals drove the stock to significant outperformance since the pandemic, we can see that it's been treading water for about a year, with the primary reason being simply valuation.

I've been covering Chipotle on Seeking Alpha for nearly two years, and although it's a sizeable position in my personal portfolio, I've maintained a 'Hold' rating on the stock for the majority of the time, as I argued that despite its impeccable business, the valuation wasn't attractive enough to justify a 'Buy'.

The sole outlier though, was an October 2023 article, and I don't want to pat myself on the back here, but I don't think I could have timed that 'Buy' and downgrade to 'Hold' better.

So, as the title suggests, I'm upgrading the stock to a 'Buy', and I don't take that lightly. Let's go into why.

Fourth-Quarter Results Demonstrate Consistency In The Post-Niccol Era

In August 2024, we got news that Chipotle's legendary CEO, Brian Niccol, is leaving to join Starbucks. My immediate reaction was that after hearing his replacement, Scott Boatwright, I have zero worries about the continuation of Chipotle's winning model. And, the fourth quarter results gave me further confidence.

In Q4-24, Chipotle's revenues were $2.84 billion, up 13.1% Y/Y. Growth was driven by same-store sales of 5.4%, of which 4.0% was traffic and 1.4% was average check. The other 7.7% was driven by unit growth.

Chipotle capped off another year of industry-leading comparable sales growth, leading to record average restaurant sales, surpassing $3.2 million, on track to the $4 million target set by the new CEO.

The strong same-store figures were accompanied by the continued rapid pace of openings, with 289 net new locations in 2024, reflecting 8.4% unit growth. This is the sixth consecutive year of unit growth acceleration, and it is expected to continue in 2025.

Meanwhile, despite inflationary pressures in beef and tougher comps because of the brisket LTO, as well as investments in portions, Chipotle kept its leading restaurant-level profitability, coming in slightly below 25% in the quarter.

Although this reflects a 60 bps drop from last year and caps off a year of no margin expansion, this was still better than expected, and quite impressive considering Chipotle was able to maintain the post-pandemic record levels.

So, a better-than-expected quarter despite your legendary CEO leaving in the middle of a PR crisis, is quite impressive in my book. Also, they made significant progress with operational projects, achieved the lowest manager turnover in Chipotle's history, and had a successful test with Honey Chicken.

Favorable Setup For 2025

Looking ahead, I believe the market's expectations for 2025 are too low, and I expect a year of multiple 'Beat & Raises'.

Management set their guide for comps in the low to mid-single-digit range, citing a weak start to January. However, this is something we've heard from almost everybody in the sector, citing unfavorable weather conditions, tougher comps, and a shift in holiday timing leading to a weak December (which also justified more cautiousness). Also, there's the impact of leap year and elections.

They expect comps to accelerate throughout the year, and I estimate we will start at 2%-3% comps in Q1, and accelerate to 5%-6% in Q4. The most important thing is that expectations are even lower than that, leaving room for upside surprises. And, on the unit expansion front, we should see about an 8% contribution to growth.

On margins, management guided for a meaningful sequential improvement in Q1, but it will still be lower than Q1-24. I estimate margins end up stable year over year, also gradually improving from quarter to quarter.

So, while I expect ~13% revenue growth, with stable margins and buybacks leading to ~18% EPS growth, the consensus is at 12% and 15%.

Valuation

We can narrow down stock performance to two factors. The first is earnings revisions (which we discussed), and the second is multiple expansion.

Regarding the latter, at $53 a share, Chipotle is trading at 41 times forward earnings, and 34 times '26 estimates.

This puts the stock close to the lowest valuation it's been over the past three years, and not coincidentally, this is even below the multiple I saw back in the 'Buy' article in October.

Conclusion

Chipotle is back at attractive levels, trading nearly 20% below the June 2024 highs, as investors are worried about lower margins and slower growth in 2025.

We are in some kind of transition period for Chipotle, with the new CEO, investing in portions, and supposedly a little more cautious consumer environment.

What's key to understanding is that at 3,700 locations and very little international presence, Chipotle's trajectory remains essentially the same and will remain such for the next decade. We are very, very far from a slowdown.

So while the stock can fluctuate along with near-term sentiment, long-term investors should closely look for truly attractive opportunities to join this long-term high-teens earnings growth ride.

One of those opportunities is right now, which leads me to upgrade Chipotle to a 'Buy'.

data.title: Oil Update - February 2025
data.description:
Once again, my expectation for March West Texas Intermediate (WTI) oil prices are the same as they were for January and February — that is, $67.50 to $77.50 per barrel. Prices stayed within my expected range during February.

I do not have many comments to make this month, as not much has changed. While some may be bullish, I am more cautious because of consumer sentiment. In fact, on February 25, the Conference Board issued a press release “US Consumer Confidence Dropped Sharply in February” that stated:

The Conference Board Consumer Confidence Index ® declined by 7.0 points in February to 98.3 (1985=100). The Present Situation Index — based on consumers’ assessment of current business and labor market conditions — fell 3.4 points to 136.5. The Expectations Index — based on consumers’ short-term outlook for income, business, and labor market conditions — dropped 9.3 points to 72.9. For the first time since June 2024, the Expectations Index was below the threshold of 80 that usually signals a recession ahead. The cutoff date for preliminary results was February 19, 2025.

With tariff uncertainty and government cutbacks, Americans are feeling cautious. Furthermore, Europe is undergoing geopolitical adjustments. Because of poor consumer sentiment, I do not expect WTI prices to exceed my expected range for the next month.

Although Eric Nuttall of Ninepoint Partners is not making a call for March, he does provide his bullish take in this half-hour YouTube, “The Macro Setup for Oil & Gas Has Never Been More Bullish | Ninepoint Energy Market Update.”

He makes a strong case for bullish oil prices and remaining invested in oil and gas stocks.

A February 27 Reuters article “Sanctions, tariffs make OPEC+ hesitant on April oil hike, sources say” suggests that no decision whether to increase production has been reached.

LONDON/MOSCOW, Feb 27 (Reuters) – OPEC+ is debating whether to raise oil output in April as planned or freeze it as its members struggle to read the global supply picture because of fresh U.S. sanctions on Venezuela, Iran and Russia, eight OPEC+ sources said.

OPEC+ usually confirms its supply policy one month in advance to have time to allocate crude to buyers. Hence, the group has until March 5-7 to finalise its April production but no consensus has emerged so far, some of the sources said.

Given the soft outlook and geopolitical uncertainty, I would be surprised if OPEC+ decided to raise production.

I expect that oil prices should be stable for the month of March.
